flashcards.back: A digital image is a representation of a two-dimensional image as a finite set of digital values called pixels, stored in binary format for computer processing.

flashcards.front: Define a pixel in digital imaging.
flashcards.back: Pixel (picture element) is the smallest unit of a digital image. Each pixel has a specific location and numerical value representing color/intensity at that point.
Sampling is the process of dividing an continuous image into a discrete grid of pixels. It determines the spatial resolution of the digital image.
Quantization assigns discrete intensity values to each sampled pixel. It determines the number of gray levels (bit depth) in the image, e.g., 8-bit = 256 gray levels.

flashcards.front: List three types of digital images.
flashcards.back: Binary (1-bit, black/white), Grayscale (8-bit, 256 gray levels), Color (24-bit RGB, 16.7 million colors).

flashcards.front: What is dynamic range in images?
flashcards.back: Dynamic range is the ratio between maximum and minimum pixel intensity values. It represents the contrast capability of an imaging system.
4-connected neighbors are pixels adjacent horizontally and vertically (N, S, E, W). Used in basic connectivity analysis.
8-connected neighbors include all 8 surrounding pixels (N, S, E, W, NE, NW, SE, SW). Used in diagonal connectivity.

flashcards.front: Define Euclidean distance between two pixels.
flashcards.back: Euclidean distance d(p,q) = √[(x1-x2)² + (y1-y2)²]. It gives the actual geometric distance in continuous space.

flashcards.front: Define Manhattan distance between two pixels.
flashcards.back: Manhattan distance d(p,q) = |x1-x2| + |y1-y2|. Also called city-block distance; sum of absolute differences.

flashcards.front: What is the RGB color model?
flashcards.back: RGB is an additive color model using Red, Green, Blue primary colors. Colors are formed by combining different intensities of these three channels.

flashcards.front: What is a histogram in image processing?
flashcards.back: A histogram is a graph showing the distribution of pixel intensity values. X-axis represents intensity levels, Y-axis shows frequency of each level.

flashcards.front: What are bit planes of an image?
flashcards.back: Bit planes are individual binary images representing each bit position in the pixel's binary representation. Higher-order planes contain more visual information.
Resolution refers to the level of detail an image holds, determined by the number of pixels (spatial resolution) and bits per pixel (intensity resolution).
BMP (uncompressed), JPEG (lossy compression), PNG (lossless with transparency), GIF (animated, 256 colors), TIFF (professional).
